text: Despite the rapid proliferation of carbon dot (CD) research, the fundamental mechanisms governing their synthesis remain a subject of intense debate. This study addresses this gap by employing a one-step hydrothermal method to produce highly efficient, blue-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from 4-aminoantipyrine. By systematically varying the reaction time from 3 to 21 hours, we investigated the structural evolution and formation pathway of the NCDs using a multi-spectroscopic approach, including FT-IR, 1H-NMR, 13C-NMR, and UV-visible spectroscopy. Our findings reveal that as hydrothermal treatment progresses, the aromatic character of the precursor diminishes due to enhanced noncovalent $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking and the hydrolysis of the pyrazole ring. This process leads to the formation of an amorphous carbon core (particle size ~5 nm) progressively encapsulated by polar aliphatic functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine moieties. Notably, this structural transformation correlates with a steady increase in photoluminescent quantum yield. Characterized by HR-TEM and XRD, these NCDs demonstrate excellent water solubility and a graphitic lattice spacing of 0.26 nm. This work provides a comprehensive mechanical insight into CD formation and offers a scalable, cost-effective route for synthesizing high-quality NCDs for diverse optoelectronic and biological applications.
